title: Candide — The Garden and the Work
subtitle: Pangloss as the false guru of the pure path without the impure, Eldorado as the pure path glimpsed but not entered, and the garden as the alchemical coagulation of daily practice

summary.one_line: Voltaire's Candide read through the Śaiva lens of the upāya-s: Pangloss as the false teacher of the pure path who has never traversed the impure, Eldorado as the śāmbhavopāya glimpsed but not enter...
summary.scope: Covers the full text of Candide (1759), with primary focus on Pangloss's philosophical trajectory, the Eldorado episode, the cumulative suffering of the characters, and the famous concluding maxim.

body.text: [See Candide ch.1-30 throughout. Pangloss is the false guru — the one who teaches Leibnizian optimism ('all is for the best in the best of all possible worlds') as a doctrine of the pure path while never having traversed the impure. He has not integrated a single experience: he contracts syphilis (the impure manifesting through the body), is hanged, dissected, enslaved, and yet continues to recite the same formula. He is the vidyeśvara without śaktipāta — the one who has the teaching but not the transmission. His philosophy is the pure path (śāmbhavopāya) asserted as dogma rather than realised as experience. The comedy of Pangloss is the comedy of the armchair nondualist who has all the words and none of the transformation. Voltaire's satire of Leibniz is at the same time a devastating critique of the pure-path teacher who has not done the work of the impure path.]

[See Candide ch.17-18. Eldorado is the pure path — the realm where the tattvas are already in their original perfection, where there is no contraction, no suffering, no need for the impure work. The streets are paved with gold and precious stones (the gross elements refined to their subtlest state); the people live in contentment and equality (no ahaṃkāra, no rāga); there is no prison, no court, no dogma (no kañcukas). Candide and Cacambo are told they may stay but choose to leave — a choice that mirrors the soul's refusal of the pure path because it has not yet exhausted the impure. 'If we stay here we shall be like everyone else' — the soul wants distinction, wants to carry something back, wants the work. Eldorado is the pure consciousness that the bound soul can glimpse but cannot inhabit because the work is not yet complete. The gold they take crumbles to dust: the pure path cannot be carried into the impure world as currency.]

[See Candide ch.30. 'Il faut cultiver notre jardin' — the final line of the novel, the alchemical coagulation of all preceding suffering into a single principle. The garden is not a metaphor for a quiet life; it is the alchemical vas (the vessel), the bounded field in which the work of transformation is done. After 30 chapters of catastrophe, displacement, and the collapse of every philosophical system, the characters settle into the simple act of cultivation. Each has a function: Candide gardens, Cunégonde bakes, Pangloss teaches (still uselessly), the old woman tends. The garden is the sādhana — the daily practice that does not solve the problem of suffering but transforms it. The work is not the attainment of enlightenment; the work is the work. Every day the earth must be turned; every day the seed must be planted; every day the weeds must be pulled. This is the alchemical nigredo through albedo to rubedo: the garden that feeds the community is the philosopher's stone made practical.]

[See Candide throughout, especially ch.2-14. The cumulative suffering of Candide's journey is tapas — the heat of experience that burns through the sheaths of illusion. Candide is beaten by the Bulgars, witnesses the Lisbon earthquake, is separated from Cunégonde, kills in self-defence (twice), is robbed, betrayed, and cheated across three continents. Each loss strips away another layer: Pangloss's philosophy (the first sheath — the intellectual covering), the memory of Eldorado (the second — the bliss covering), the hope of reunion with Cunégonde (the third — the will covering). By the time he reaches the farm, he has been stripped to the bare self: no philosophy, no hope, no illusion. The garden is what remains when tapas has burned everything else. This is the Śaiva model of the path: the suffering is not the problem; the refusal to let suffering transform you is the problem. Candide's arc is the soul that finally stops asking 'why' and starts doing.]

gaps: Pangloss as a type could be compared with the Grand Inquisitor and other false gurus
More detailed reading of Eldorado's political/economic structures as dharma
The garden could be read against the Tantric concept of the kula (family/community of practitioners)
Episode-by-episode mapping of each disaster to a specific sheath or impurity

issues.title: All passages are meta-commentary — need primary text extraction
issues.description: Passages currently describe the esoteric mapping. Real passages should include actual quotations from the novel with analysis.

issues.title: Cacambo as the practical guide
issues.description: Cacambo, Candide's servant, functions as the pragmatic assistant — the one who navigates the actual world while Candide theorises. He is the karmendriya to Candide's jnanendriya. Not yet developed.

versions.summary: Initial RO created mapping Voltaire's Candide onto the Śaiva upāya framework. Four passages covering Pangloss as false guru, Eldorado as the pure path glimpsed, the garden as the daily sādhana, and suffering as tapas.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 1 primary source and 4 passages
